MRI brain: signal abnormality in hypothalamus and brainstem. PET: hypermetabolic mesenteric nodes. Small bowel capsule endoscopy: diffuse white shaggy villi throughout jejunum.

lab_results.CMP: Albumin 1.6 (critically low). Cholesterol low (low — fat malabsorption). Normal LFTs.
lab_results.Specialty: Small bowel biopsy with PAS stain: Lamina propria densely packed with foamy macrophages containing PAS-positive, diastase-resistant intracellular granules — organisms resist normal phagolysosomal digestion. Villous blunting with lymphatic dilation. Electron microscopy: Trilamellar cell wall organisms (1-2µm) within macrophages. PCR of duodenal biopsy: Tropheryma whipplei DNA — POSITIVE (specific). CSF PCR: Tropheryma whipplei — POSITIVE (CNS involvement confirmed). Stool culture: Negative for pathogens. HIV: Negative.

hx_brief: 44yo female with 10 months of joint swelling and morning stiffness is referred to rheumatology. Her PCP has been treating her for 'reactive arthritis' with NSAIDs and physical therapy.

hints.hx: Morning stiffness lasting 2-3 hours — she cannot close her hands into a fist until midmorning. Bilateral wrist, MCP, and PIP swelling — symmetric. Fatigue profound. Noticed a small firm nodule on her right elbow 2 months ago. No psoriatic skin or nail changes. No uveitis. No preceding infection. No DIP or spine involvement. No photosensitivity. Smokes 10 cigarettes/day — identified risk factor. Mother and maternal grandmother had similar joint disease.
hints.pe: Symmetric synovitis — bilateral MCPs (2nd-5th), PIPs, and wrists bilaterally. Early ulnar deviation of right hand. DIP joints: normal — key distinguishing feature from osteoarthritis and psoriatic arthritis. Rheumatoid nodule on right olecranon — firm, non-tender. No skin rash. No iritis. No lymphadenopathy.
hints.img: X-ray bilateral hands and wrists: Periarticular osteopenia at MCPs and wrists (early). Marginal erosions at 2nd and 3rd MCP heads bilaterally. No joint space narrowing yet — early structural damage. Musculoskeletal ultrasound: Power Doppler signal (blood flow) within synovium bilaterally — active synovitis confirmed. Synovial hypertrophy grade 2.

lab_results.CBC: Hgb 10.6 (low) — normocytic anemia of chronic disease. Thrombocytosis 560,000 (high — reactive).
lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: Anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide): 480 U/mL (markedly positive; >3x ULN — highly specific for RA, present before erosions). RF IgM: 320 IU/mL (elevated). CRP: 68 mg/L. ESR: 94mm/hr. ANA: Negative. Anti-dsDNA: Negative. Anti-Sm: Negative. ACPA positivity + RF positivity = seropositive RA (worst prognosis, most erosive). DAS28-CRP: 6.4 (high disease activity). Treatment: Methotrexate + short-course prednisone bridge. Smoking cessation mandatory (smoking drives ACPA production and worsens outcomes).

hx_brief: 48yo male with known bicuspid aortic valve presents to cardiology follow-up after his wife insisted. 'I've had the valve issue since I was a kid — they always say watch and wait.'

hints.hx: Bicuspid aortic valve diagnosed at age 12. Last echo 4 years ago showed 'mild to moderate regurgitation — continue monitoring.' He's been 'too busy' for follow-up since. Now sleeps on 3 pillows, gets winded walking to his car, and his wife says his heart 'pounds' at night. He's gained 6 pounds but attributes it to less exercise. Occasional awareness of his heartbeat when lying on his left side. No syncope. No chest pain.
hints.pe: BP 162/52 — wide pulse pressure 110mmHg. HR 90. Bounding 'water-hammer' pulses at wrists. Head bobs with each heartbeat (de Musset sign). Capillary pulsations visible in nail beds (Quincke pulse). Blowing early diastolic decrescendo murmur at left sternal border — best in held expiration leaning forward. PMI: hyperdynamic, displaced to left anterior axillary line.
hints.img: Echo: Severe AR — vena contracta 9mm. Regurgitant fraction 62%. LV end-diastolic dimension 76mm (severely dilated). EF 38% — new reduction (was 62% four years ago — decompensating). Aortic root 5.2cm. Ascending aorta 5.4cm (aneurysmal — bicuspid aortopathy). Austin Flint murmur confirmed on echo (AR jet hits anterior MV leaflet causing mid-diastolic rumble mimicking MS).

lab_results.CMP: BNP 920 pg/mL (high — LV decompensation). Creatinine 1.2.
lab_results.EKG: LVH with strain pattern. Sinus tachycardia. Prolonged PR 240ms.
lab_results.Trop: 0.10 ng/mL (high) — elevated from LV wall stress.
lab_results.Specialty: Cardiac catheterization: Gorlin formula — regurgitant fraction 60%. LVEDP 28mmHg (elevated — failing LV). CO 3.4 L/min (reduced). No significant CAD. Aortic root 5.2cm confirmed. Surgical indication: EF <50% AND/OR LVIDd >65mm AND/OR symptoms. All three criteria met. Bentall procedure planned (aortic valve + aortic root replacement with graft) — not TAVR, given young age and root aneurysm requiring surgical repair. Four-year delay without monitoring allowed silent decompensation.

hx_brief: 42yo male with a history of IV drug use presents with 8 weeks of progressive lower back pain and new onset difficulty walking upstairs. He was discharged from the ED twice with NSAIDs and 'muscle strain.'

hints.hx: Active heroin use (IV). Back pain is constant, worse at night, not position-dependent — pathological pattern. Started in the lumbar region, now also has thoracic pain. Cannot climb stairs without bilateral leg weakness and buckling. Had dental work 3 months ago. Fevers and sweats for 6 weeks. Has not seen a doctor regularly. No bowel or bladder symptoms yet — but hesitant to admit.
hints.pe: Temperature 39.1C. Point tenderness L3-L4 vertebral bodies on percussion. Lower extremity weakness 3/5 bilaterally — proximal more than distal. DTRs hyperreflexic bilaterally (UMN). Bilateral Babinski positive. Perianal sensation mildly reduced. Urinary hesitancy — post-void residual 280mL.
hints.img: MRI spine with gadolinium (STAT): L3-L4 vertebral endplate destruction. Disc space narrowing and T2 signal in disc. Rim-enhancing epidural collection L3-L5 — compressing thecal sac. Cord signal change at conus — myelopathy. Extent of epidural abscess: 4 vertebral levels. TEE ordered.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 26,800 (high) with 92% PMNs. Bands 20% (high). ESR 132mm/hr (high). CRP 220mg/L (high).
lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: Blood cultures x2 (drawn before antibiotics): MRSA (methicillin-resistant S. aureus) — grown at 18 hours. TEE: 1.6cm vegetation on tricuspid valve — concurrent infective endocarditis (right-sided, classic for IV drug users). CT-guided disc aspiration: MRSA — same strain. Mechanism: IV drug use → bacteremia → hematogenous seeding of vertebral endplates (Batson venous plexus) → discitis/osteomyelitis → contiguous epidural abscess. Treatment: Vancomycin 6 weeks IV. Surgical decompression emergent (cord compression with myelopathy).

hx_brief: 16yo female is brought to the ED by her mother after fainting at her high school track meet. She ran the 400m, finished the race, then collapsed in the infield. She is adamant she is 'completely fine' and wants to go home.

hints.hx: She is a varsity track athlete. Mother reports she has been eating 'very clean' for 9 months — eliminated all processed food, all carbohydrates, and has been running 2-a-day practices. Lost approximately 28 pounds over this period. Mother has noticed she wears baggy clothes now and avoids family meals with excuses. Periods stopped 5 months ago — patient says 'that's normal for athletes.' Fine downy hair appearing on her arms. She denies restricting, purging, or excessive exercise — says she is 'training like the other girls.' Mood: defensive and irritable during interview.
hints.pe: BP 84/56, HR 48. Temp 36.0C. BMI 14.8 — severely underweight. Appears thin in hospital gown. Lanugo on bilateral forearms and upper back. Parotid glands: mildly enlarged bilaterally. Teeth: enamel erosion on posterior surfaces. Skin: dry, sallow. Extremities: cool, mild peripheral cyanosis. No edema. Orthostatic BP drop 22mmHg.
hints.img: ECG: Sinus bradycardia 48bpm. QTc 498ms (prolonged — arrhythmia risk from electrolyte abnormalities). Low voltage. No ST changes. Echo: Mild mitral valve prolapse (from loss of cardiac muscle mass). Small pericardial effusion. EF preserved. Bone density scan: Z-score -2.4 at lumbar spine (osteoporosis in a 16-year-old).

lab_results.CBC: WBC 2.6 (low — leukopenia). Hgb 8.8 (low — normocytic). Platelets 68,000 (low) — pancytopenia from starvation-induced marrow suppression.
lab_results.CMP: Na 128 (low). K 2.4 (critically low — dangerous hypokalemia, arrhythmia risk). Glucose 38 (critically low). Phosphorus 1.2 (low — refeeding syndrome risk). Albumin 2.0 (critically low). Cholesterol 306 (high — paradoxically elevated from abnormal lipid metabolism in starvation).
lab_results.EKG: Sinus bradycardia 36bpm. QTc 524ms (critically prolonged — risk of Torsades de Pointes). ST depression V4-V6.
lab_results.UA: Specific gravity 1.003 (low — maximally dilute, inability to concentrate). Ketones 4+ (high).
lab_results.Specialty: LH: 0.4 mIU/mL (suppressed). FSH: 0.6 mIU/mL (suppressed). Estradiol: <10 pg/mL (undetectable). IGF-1: markedly low. Cortisol: 32 µg/dL (elevated — stress response). Prolactin: Low. Hypothalamic amenorrhea from caloric restriction. T3: 0.4 ng/mL (low — euthyroid sick syndrome from starvation). TSH: Normal (central adaptation). Confirmed anorexia nervosa — restricting type (purging denied initially but parotid enlargement suggests mixed type).

hx_brief: 54yo male with a history of alcohol use disorder and liver cirrhosis presents with 2 months of increasing abdominal girth, bilateral leg edema, and confusion that his wife says comes and goes.

hints.hx: Last drink was 2 years ago. Cirrhosis diagnosed 4 years ago (Child-Pugh B). On propranolol and lactulose. Abdominal swelling despite compliance with sodium restriction and spironolactone. Confusion episodes: gets lost going to the bathroom at night, occasionally doesn't recognize his son. Mild tremor. No fever. No abdominal pain. Stools have been darker than usual for 2 weeks.
hints.pe: Jaundiced. Muscle wasting — temporal and bilateral thenar atrophy. Asterixis — bilateral, grade 2. Spider angiomata on chest and arms. Palmar erythema. Caput medusae. Abdomen: distended, fluid wave present — large ascites. Liver: non-palpable (shrunken). Spleen: palpable. Bilateral 3+ pitting edema to knees. Guaiac positive stool.
hints.img: CT Abdomen: Nodular liver — cirrhosis. Large ascites. Splenomegaly 16cm. Dilated portal vein 16mm. Varices — gastric and esophageal. No hepatic mass. Upper endoscopy: Grade 3 esophageal varices with red wale markings — high bleeding risk. No active bleeding.

lab_results.CMP: Total bilirubin 4.8 (high). Direct 3.2 (high). Albumin 2.0 (critically low). INR 2.4 (high — synthetic dysfunction). Creatinine 1.6 (high). Na 128 (low). Ammonia 142 µmol/L (high) — encephalopathy.
lab_results.UA: Urine Na <10 mEq/L — avid sodium retention (hepatorenal physiology).
lab_results.Specialty: Diagnostic paracentesis: SAAG 1.6 g/dL (≥1.1 — portal hypertension confirmed). Total ascites protein: 1.2 g/dL. Ascites WBC: 180 cells/µL (PMN 120 — below 250 PMN threshold, not yet SBP). Culture: No growth. Hepatitis serology: IgM anti-HAV: POSITIVE (acute infection). IgG anti-HAV: Positive (confirms active primary infection, not prior immunity alone). HBsAg: Positive (chronic carrier — superinfection). HCV: Negative. AFP: 24 ng/mL — elevated, hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance required given cirrhosis.

hx_brief: 47yo male with longstanding GERD presents to GI for routine surveillance endoscopy. He mentions 'I feel fine — I just take my omeprazole and forget about it.'

hints.hx: GERD since age 28. Barrett's esophagus (short segment, low-grade dysplasia) found 3 years ago at his last surveillance scope. Has been 'too busy' for the recommended annual follow-up — this is his first scope in 3 years. Lost 11 pounds over the past 8 months — he attributes it to 'finally watching what I eat.' New dysphagia for solid foods for 3 months that he didn't mention until directly asked. Occasional hematemesis dismissed as 'acid reflux.' 30 pack-year smoking history.
hints.pe: BMI 22 (down from 28 three years ago). Mildly pale. No supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No hepatomegaly. No ascites. Mild epigastric tenderness on deep palpation.
hints.img: EGD: At the GEJ — irregular raised mucosal lesion 3.4cm, contact bleeding, irregular nodularity. Extends 2cm above GEJ. Biopsies taken. CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Wall thickening at GEJ/distal esophagus. Three enlarged regional lymph nodes (largest 1.8cm). Liver: no metastases. PET: Uptake at primary and regional nodes only.

lab_results.CMP: Albumin 3.0. CEA: 12.4 (mildly elevated). CA 19-9: Normal. HER2/neu: Sent.
lab_results.Specialty: EGD biopsy (upper-mid esophagus, 24cm from incisors): Poorly differentiated carcinoma. Immunohistochemistry: p40 positive, CK5/6 positive, TTF-1 negative, CDX2 negative. Keratin pearl formation on H&E. Individual cell keratinization. No glandular differentiation identified. Barrett's metaplasia: Absent. H. pylori: Negative. PD-L1 CPS: 14. HER2: Negative. SCC antigen: 3.8 ng/mL (elevated). Staging: cT2N1M0. EUS: Tumor invades muscularis propria, 2 periesophageal nodes enlarged.

hx_brief: 34yo female presents to the ED 6 days postpartum with acute chest pain and severe shortness of breath. She had an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. No prior cardiac history. Her obstetrician told her 'postpartum chest pain is usually just musculoskeletal.'

hints.hx: Chest pain onset was sudden, during an argument with her husband. Severe pressure, radiating to the left arm. She is breastfeeding and reports extreme emotional stress since delivery. No fever. No leg swelling. No prior hypertension. No cocaine use. No smoking. No family history of cardiac disease. She is otherwise healthy, 34yo, no risk factors. The timing — emotional stressor triggering acute chest pain — is the key.
hints.pe: Diaphoretic, anxious. BP 162/104, HR 118. Bilateral fine crackles at lung bases. S3 gallop audible. JVD present. No leg swelling. No calf tenderness. Abdomen: normal postpartum uterus.
hints.img: Echo: Apical ballooning — the apex of the LV is akinetic and balloons outward, while the base contracts normally. This is the reverse of an ischemic pattern (LAD territory would be mid and apical, but BASE would be spared, not hyperkinetic). LVEF 25%. No pericardial effusion. Wall motion abnormality extends beyond any single coronary territory — critical finding.

lab_results.CMP: Normal.
lab_results.EKG: ST elevation in V3-V5, T-wave inversions diffusely. QTc prolonged 510ms.
lab_results.Trop: Troponin I 18.4 ng/mL (high — markedly elevated, but disproportionately LOW relative to degree of wall motion abnormality — hallmark of Takotsubo).
lab_results.Specialty: Urgent coronary angiography: LAD, LCX, RCA — all patent, no stenosis >20%, no thrombus, no dissection, no plaque rupture. TIMI 3 flow throughout. Left ventriculography: Apical akinesis with basal hypercontractility — wall motion abnormality extends beyond any single coronary artery territory. RV: Normal. Intravascular OCT: No intimal tear, no intramural hematoma. Urine catecholamines: Epinephrine 4x upper limit of normal. Norepinephrine: 2.8x ULN. EF on ventriculography: 22%. Repeat echo at 72hrs: EF improved to 35% — partial recovery already.

hx_brief: 33yo male presents to the ED for the third time in 10 weeks. His PCP has treated him twice for 'infectious gastroenteritis' with empiric antibiotics and oral rehydration. He is now 16 pounds lighter, cannot work, and his third stool culture just came back negative.

hints.hx: Up to 12 bloody stools per day — the blood is mixed with the stool, not just on the surface. Urgency so severe he cannot defer more than a few minutes. Crampy lower abdominal pain that temporarily improves after defecation. No nausea, no vomiting, no fever currently (had fever during first two episodes). He recently developed bilateral knee pain and a red eye his optometrist called 'iritis.' No travel. No sick contacts. No antibiotics before this illness started. Family history: sister has 'some kind of gut problem.'
hints.pe: Thin, pale. BP 104/68, HR 102. Abdomen: left lower quadrant tenderness, no guarding, no peritoneal signs. Rectal exam: bright red blood on glove. Eyes: left anterior uveitis — ciliary flush. Knees: mild effusion bilateral without warmth. Skin: two tender red nodules on bilateral shins.
hints.img: AXR: Colonic wall thickening visible on plain film — loss of haustral markings in the descending and sigmoid colon. No toxic megacolon (transverse colon <6cm). CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: Continuous mucosal thickening from rectum to splenic flexure — no skip lesions. Mucosal enhancement. Pericolonic stranding. No perforation. No abscess.

lab_results.CMP: Albumin 1.8 (critically low). CRP 118 mg/L (high). ESR 128mm/hr (high).
lab_results.UA: Normal — uveitis is extraintestinal, not renal.
lab_results.Specialty: Biopsies: Crypt architectural distortion. Crypt abscesses (neutrophilic cryptitis). Basal plasmacytosis — key finding distinguishing IBD from infectious colitis. Paneth cell metaplasia in rectum. No granulomas — rules out Crohn's. p-ANCA: Positive. ASCA: Negative. Fecal calprotectin: 2,400 µg/g. C. diff negative x3. CMV staining negative. Mayo endoscopic score 3 (severe). Extraintestinal manifestations: uveitis, ankle arthritis, oral ulcers, erythema nodosum — all indicate active UC.

hx_brief: 69yo male is brought to his PCP by his daughter who says 'he just isn't the same person he was a year ago.' He thinks she's 'overreacting.'

hints.hx: Progressive memory loss — repeats the same stories 5-6 times in an hour. Got lost driving to his barbershop he's used for 30 years. Has been accusing his wife of hiding his belongings. Word-finding pauses are frequent. But crucially — no gait disturbance, no incontinence, no falls. No Parkinsonism symptoms. No visual hallucinations. No rapid decline. He had a MMSE 2 years ago of 28/30 at his prior PCP — attributed to 'testing anxiety.'
hints.pe: Fully ambulatory with normal gait and balance. No tremor, no rigidity. MMSE today: 16/30 (recall 0/3, orientation 4/6, serial 7s failed, language 4/5). MoCA: 11/30. Frontal lobe release signs: grasp reflex present. No focal neurologic deficits. No papilledema.
hints.img: MRI Brain with volumetrics: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy — entorhinal cortex volume 2.5 SD below age-matched norms. Posterior cortical thinning. No significant white matter disease. No mass. No hydrocephalus. Amyloid PET: Diffuse cortical amyloid — frontal, parietal, temporal, cingulate. Tau PET: Bilateral hippocampal and lateral temporal tau deposition.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: CSF: Aβ42: 220 pg/mL (severely reduced). p-tau 181: 128 pg/mL (markedly elevated). Total tau: 820 pg/mL (elevated). Aβ42/p-tau 181 ratio: 1.7 (abnormal; <6.5 indicates AD). ApoE: ε4/ε3 (heterozygous — 3x population risk). Neurodegeneration biomarkers confirm amyloid (A+) and tau pathology (T+). Workup: TSH, B12, RPR, HIV — all normal. Urine heavy metals — normal. Lewy body antibody panel: Negative.  Lecanemab or donanemab trial eligibility screening initiated (anti-amyloid immunotherapy).

hx_brief: 71yo male is brought to neurology by his wife because 'his walking changed and he's been forgetting things for the past year.' He had a neurological evaluation 6 months ago — diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease. She's seeking a second opinion.

hints.hx: Gait changed FIRST — small shuffling steps, episodes of feet freezing — then cognitive decline developed 3 months later. Now urinary incontinence — 'can't make it to the bathroom in time.' All three symptoms progressively worsening. No tremor at rest. No Parkinsonian features. Had a 'brain bleed' (subarachnoid hemorrhage from ruptured aneurysm — coiled) 11 months ago. The prior Alzheimer's diagnosis missed the key temporal sequence and prior SAH history.
hints.pe: Gait: magnetic — feet appear glued, very small steps, wide-based, en-bloc turning. Cognitive: MMSE 21/30 — frontal and executive function most impaired. No resting tremor. No cogwheel rigidity. No Parkinsonian features. Frontal release: grasp and snout reflexes present. Normal cranial nerves. Urinary urgency incontinence confirmed.
hints.img: MRI Brain: Markedly enlarged ventricles — Evans' ratio 0.41 (severely abnormal; normal <0.30). Corpus callosum stretched (thinning on sagittal view). Periventricular FLAIR hyperintensity — transependymal CSF flow. No sulcal atrophy disproportionate to ventricular enlargement. No amyloid on PET (performed at outside facility for Alzheimer's workup — negative, supporting against AD).

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: LP: Opening pressure 16 cmH2O (upper normal). CSF: Clear, colorless. Protein 48mg/dL, glucose normal, no cells. AD biomarkers: Aβ42 normal, p-tau normal — rules out Alzheimer's pathologically. Large-volume tap (50mL removed): Gait timed 10-meter walk BEFORE: 32 seconds. AFTER (1 hour): 14 seconds (56% improvement). MMSE improved 5 points. Urinary urgency improved. Tap test strongly positive — predicts excellent VP shunt response. Prior SAH caused communicating hydrocephalus (impaired CSF reabsorption at arachnoid granulations). Misdiagnosed as Alzheimer's — key: gait first, then cognition (opposite of AD where cognition precedes gait changes).

hx_brief: 29yo male presents to a walk-in clinic with mild intermittent jaundice that has been occurring since college. He has been told 'your liver tests are normal — you're fine' on three separate occasions.

hints.hx: Jaundice appears after prolonged fasting (he's preparing for a colonoscopy now — 3-day clear liquid diet), after viral illnesses, after heavy exercise, and after skipping meals during finals week. Each episode lasts 2-4 days then resolves completely. First noticed at age 20 during a fasting religious holiday. He otherwise feels completely well. No alcohol, no medications, no hepatitis exposures. His uncle had 'some liver thing — benign.'
hints.pe: Mild scleral icterus — the only finding. Skin has slight yellow tinge. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No spider angiomata. No Kayser-Fleischer rings. Abdomen completely normal. Neurologically intact.
hints.img: RUQ ultrasound: Liver — normal size, echogenicity, and texture. No gallstones. No biliary dilation. No portal hypertension. Liver elastography (Fibroscan): E 4.2 kPa (normal — no fibrosis).

lab_results.CBC: Normal — reticulocyte count 1.2% (normal). Haptoglobin: 88 mg/dL (normal). Direct Coombs: Negative. Peripheral smear: No spherocytes, no schistocytes, no elliptocytes — hemolysis ruled out.
lab_results.CMP: Total bilirubin 4.8 mg/dL (high). Direct 0.4 mg/dL (normal). Indirect 4.4 mg/dL (high). ALT 18, AST 22, ALP 62, GGT 14 — all completely normal.
lab_results.UA: Urine: No bilirubin (conjugated bilirubin would appear in urine — its absence confirms exclusively unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia). Urobilinogen: Normal.
lab_results.Specialty: UGT1A1 promoter genotyping: Homozygous (TA)7/(TA)7 repeat — '7/7' genotype (also written UGT1A1*28/*28). This repeat reduces UGT1A1 promoter activity by 70% → reduced conjugation of unconjugated bilirubin → isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia. Gilbert syndrome — benign, no treatment, no follow-up needed. Fasting, illness, stress, and exercise all increase bilirubin load and unmask the conjugation defect. No increased risk of liver disease. Important: this same variant causes severe neonatal jaundice and increases irinotecan toxicity (reduced glucuronidation of active metabolite SN-38).

hx_brief: 54yo male with a 25-year history of heavy drinking presents with 2 months of worsening shortness of breath and bilateral leg swelling. He stopped drinking 14 months ago after 'a scare' — his friend died of cirrhosis.

hints.hx: Quit drinking 14 months ago. No prior diagnosed cardiac disease. No hypertension. No diabetes. Cannot walk to the mailbox without stopping. Sleeps on 4 pillows. Wakes at 3am gasping. Bilateral ankle swelling started 4 months ago — now to mid-thigh. Intermittent palpitations. PCP attributed dyspnea to 'deconditioning from alcohol use.' Never had an echo.
hints.pe: HR 116 irregularly irregular (AF with rapid ventricular response). BP 92/62. JVP 18cm with prominent x and y descents. Bilateral crackles to mid-scapulae. S3 gallop at apex. Hepatomegaly 5cm below right costal margin — tender (congestive). Ascites: shifting dullness present. 4+ bilateral pitting edema to mid-thigh. Peripheral cyanosis.
hints.img: Echo: Four-chamber dilation. EF 12% (severely reduced). No wall motion abnormalities — global hypokinesis (dilated cardiomyopathy pattern, not ischemic). Moderate functional MR and TR. Estimated RVSP 62mmHg (secondary PH from LV failure). CXR: Massive cardiomegaly. Bilateral pleural effusions. Pulmonary vascular congestion.

lab_results.CBC: MCV 108 (high — macrocytosis from alcohol). Hgb 9.6 (low). Thrombocytopenia 98,000 (low — hypersplenism).
lab_results.CMP: Na 124 (low — dilutional hyponatremia). GGT 380 (high). AST:ALT ratio 2.8:1 (high — >2:1 alcoholic pattern). Total bilirubin 3.8 (high). Albumin 2.4 (low).
lab_results.EKG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response 126bpm. LBBB. Diffuse low voltage.
lab_results.Trop: 0.22 ng/mL (high) — chronically elevated from myocardial stress.
lab_results.ABG: pO2 58 (low) on room air. Lactate 3.2 mmol/L (high) — cardiogenic shock physiology.
lab_results.Specialty: BNP: 8,400 pg/mL (severely elevated). Thiamine level: 18 nmol/L (low — supplementation required; Wernicke's prevention). Selenium: Low (selenium deficiency is a secondary cause of dilated cardiomyopathy). EtOH: 0.0 (confirmed abstinent). Cardiac MRI (when stabilized): Diffuse myocardial fibrosis on late gadolinium enhancement — 14 months of abstinence; EF not recovered, suggesting established fibrosis rather than reversible toxic cardiomyopathy. ICD implanted (EF <35%, LBBB — CRT-D criteria met).

hx_brief: 23yo female woke up this morning unable to see properly out of her left eye and presents to the ED. She is extremely myopic and was told last week by her optometrist that her prescription had increased again.

hints.hx: Myopia -13 diopters bilaterally — extreme high myopia is the strongest risk factor for retinal detachment (axial elongation stretches retina). Three days ago noticed new floaters 'like a curtain of dust' and flashing lights in peripheral vision when moving her eyes in the dark — she Googled it and assumed it was a 'vitreous floater — benign.' This morning woke with a gray 'shadow' obscuring her inferior visual field — has progressed to involve her central vision by the time she arrives.
hints.pe: Left eye visual acuity: 20/400 (severely reduced). Right eye: 20/40 (corrected). Left eye — Pupil: RAPD (relative afferent pupillary defect — indicates significant retinal dysfunction). Fundoscopy left eye: Retina visible — elevated, billowing, gray membrane obscuring the optic disc. Loss of choroidal red reflex in superior temporal quadrant. Right eye fundoscopy: Peripheral lattice degeneration with pigmented edges — at-risk lesion.
hints.img: Ocular B-scan ultrasound: Left eye — highly reflective mobile membrane arising from posterior pole, anchored at ora serrata and optic disc. No choroidal mass. No vitreous hemorrhage. Right eye: Lattice degeneration — treated prophylactically.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: Fluorescein angiography and slit-lamp with 90D lens: Large superotemporal rhegmatogenous retinal detachment — horseshoe tear at 10 o'clock with posterior vitreous detachment. Subretinal fluid extends to involve the fovea — macular-off detachment. Prognosis: Visual recovery inversely related to duration of macular involvement — best outcomes with surgery within 24hrs of macula-off. Pars plana vitrectomy with gas tamponade performed urgently. Post-op: face-down positioning required 2 weeks. Right eye: prophylactic laser retinopexy of lattice degeneration. Key: photopsia (flashing lights) + new floaters = PVD with retinal traction — ophthalmology emergency.

hx_brief: 49yo male of Northern European descent presents with progressive fatigue, joint pain in his hands, and 'my wife says I look tan — I don't go outside much.'

hints.hx: Bronze-gray skin discoloration progressively worsening over 4 years — most prominent on face, neck, hands, and a surgical scar on his abdomen (scars hyperpigment in iron overload). Severe fatigue — sleeping 10-12 hours and still exhausted. Painful swelling of the 2nd and 3rd MCP joints bilaterally for 2 years — his rheumatologist diagnosed 'atypical RA' and started hydroxychloroquine without improvement. New-onset impotence and loss of libido for 18 months. Father died of liver cancer at 52.
hints.pe: Bronze/slate-gray hyperpigmentation — generalized. Hepatomegaly — liver 6cm below costal margin, firm. Bilateral 2nd and 3rd MCP joint enlargement — hook-like osteophytes (classic for hemochromatosis — not typical RA). Testicular atrophy. No spider angiomata. No ascites. Cardiac: irregular rhythm.
hints.img: MRI Liver: Diffuse T2 hypointensity throughout hepatic parenchyma (iron appears dark on T2 — 'black liver'). Hepatic iron concentration estimated 350 µmol/g dry weight (severely elevated; normal <36). No hepatic mass. Echocardiogram: Restrictive cardiomyopathy — impaired diastolic filling. Moderate TR. EF 48%.

lab_results.CMP: AST 92 (high), ALT 78 (high) — iron-induced hepatocellular injury. Glucose 228 (high — 'bronze diabetes'). ALP 188 (high). Albumin 3.0 (low-normal). Total bilirubin 2.4 (high).
lab_results.EKG: Atrial fibrillation (cardiac iron deposition → arrhythmia). Prolonged PR. Low voltage.
lab_results.Specialty: Serum ferritin: 6,200 ng/mL (severely elevated; normal <300 in men). Transferrin saturation: 92% (severely elevated; normal <45%). HFE gene sequencing: Homozygous C282Y mutation (p.Cys282Tyr) — classic hereditary hemochromatosis. Liver biopsy: Hepatic iron index 4.8 (>1.9 confirms hemochromatosis). Stage 3 fibrosis (periportal bridging). AFP: 24 ng/mL — hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance required (cirrhosis + hemochromatosis = very high HCC risk). Treatment: Therapeutic phlebotomy weekly until ferritin <50 ng/mL.

hx_brief: 38yo male presents with 3 months of episodic dark urine in the morning, fatigue, and two episodes of abdominal pain. His PCP found pancytopenia and referred him to hematology. He was previously healthy.

hints.hx: Dark urine is specifically in the morning — clears by afternoon. He noticed it after sleep. Two episodes of severe abdominal pain, each lasting 6-12 hours — no clear trigger. He had a DVT in his left leg 6 months ago — attributed to 'long plane flight.' He is not on anticoagulation. He has been transfused twice this year for anemia — each transfusion made him feel better briefly. He has no history of liver disease, no alcohol, no toxin exposure.
hints.pe: Pale, jaundiced sclera. BP 118/76, HR 96. Abdomen: mild splenomegaly, no hepatomegaly. No lymphadenopathy. No petechiae. Skin: mild jaundice. No ecchymoses.
hints.img: CT abdomen: Splenomegaly. Hepatic vein narrowing — early Budd-Chiari. Portal vein partially thrombosed. No liver lesions. MRI abdomen: Hepatic vein outflow obstruction confirmed — intrahepatic pattern. Bone marrow biopsy: Hypercellular marrow with normal morphology — no dysplasia, no blasts.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 2,400 (low — leukopenia). Hgb 6.8 (critically low — severe hemolytic anemia). Platelets 62,000 (low — thrombocytopenia). Pancytopenia across all lines.
lab_results.CMP: LDH 1,840 (critically high — intravascular hemolysis). Indirect bilirubin 4.2 (high). Haptoglobin undetectable (critically low). Cr 1.8 (high).
lab_results.UA: Hemoglobinuria — dark brown urine. No RBC casts.
lab_results.Coags: INR normal. D-dimer elevated (high) — thrombotic tendency.
lab_results.Specialty: Flow cytometry peripheral blood: Absent CD55 and CD59 on RBCs (52% clone size) and granulocytes (68% clone size) — GPI-anchor deficient population. Sucrose lysis test: positive (historical). Ham test: positive (historical). PIG-A gene mutation confirmed on sequencing.

hx_brief: 13yo male is brought to his pediatrician with 5 days of 'Coca-Cola colored urine' and facial puffiness that his mother treated with antihistamines for 3 days without improvement. He is 4 pounds heavier than his last visit 6 weeks ago.

hints.hx: Facial puffiness is worse in the morning and improves through the day. The dark urine started suddenly 5 days ago — no dysuria, no frequency, no fever. He had a skin infection on his left lower leg 4 weeks ago — looked like a crusty golden sore — treated only with topical ointment by his mother, not seen by a doctor. He has been mildly short of breath going up stairs but attributes it to 'being out of shape.' Blood pressure has never been checked before today. No prior kidney problems. No sore throat recently.
hints.pe: BP 158/102 (high for age — >95th percentile). Periorbital puffiness — worse on left. Mild pitting edema bilateral ankles. Lungs: crackles bilateral bases. Abdomen: mild flank tenderness bilaterally. Left lower leg: healing crusted erosion 2cm — golden-yellow crust, hyperpigmented border.
hints.img: CXR: Mild pulmonary vascular congestion. Small bilateral pleural effusions. Renal ultrasound: Bilateral kidneys enlarged for age — increased echogenicity (acute inflammation). No obstruction. No stones. Doppler: Elevated resistive indices bilaterally — consistent with acute nephropathy.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 11,800 (high). Hgb 10.8 (low — dilutional anemia from fluid retention). Normal differential.
lab_results.CMP: Creatinine 2.8 (high — elevated for age). BUN 52 (high). Na 134 (low-normal). K 5.4 (high). Bicarbonate 18 (low — metabolic acidosis from reduced GFR).
lab_results.UA: Cola-colored. RBC casts on microscopy. Dysmorphic RBCs. 4+ blood (high). 3+ protein (high). Urine protein:creatinine ratio 4.2 (high).
lab_results.Specialty: ASO titer: 1:640 (elevated — prior streptococcal infection; peaks 3-6 weeks after pharyngitis). Anti-DNase B: Markedly elevated (more sensitive for skin infections than ASO — impetigo raises anti-DNase B more reliably than ASO). Streptozyme: Positive. C3: 22 mg/dL (severely low; normal 90-180 — classic for PSGN, via alternative complement pathway). C4: Normal (distinguishes from SLE where both C3 and C4 are low). ANA: Negative. ANCA: Negative. Anti-GBM: Negative. Throat culture: Negative. Skin wound culture: GAS (group A Streptococcus).

hx_brief: 71yo male with a 3-month history of food regurgitation, halitosis, and a gurgling noise from his neck after eating presents to gastroenterology. He has lost 18 pounds.

hints.hx: Regurgitation occurs hours after meals — food comes back undigested and with no acidic taste (no stomach acid contacted it — stored in a blind pouch above the stomach). He has learned to press on the left side of his neck to 'help food go down.' Woke up twice choking on regurgitated food. No heartburn. No odynophagia. Had an aspiration pneumonia 6 weeks ago treated with antibiotics. Prior upper endoscopy 2 years ago was 'normal' — likely never visualized the diverticulum because scope passed straight into the lumen.
hints.pe: Mild halitosis despite good oral hygiene. Left posterior neck: soft, reducible swelling that produces a gurgling sound on compression (Boyce sign). No lymphadenopathy. No tracheal deviation. Lungs: residual right lower lobe crackles from aspiration pneumonia.
hints.img: Barium swallow: Posterior outpouching at pharyngoesophageal junction — above the cricopharyngeus muscle — filling with barium before the esophagus. Characteristic appearance: contrast pools in pouch. Fluoroscopy: Barium refluxes from pouch to hypopharynx, risk of aspiration. CT Neck: Posterior hypopharyngeal pouch 3.8cm — arising from Killian's dehiscence (area of muscular weakness between thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus).

lab_results.CMP: Albumin 2.4 (low — malnutrition from inadequate intake). Na 136. Otherwise normal.
lab_results.Specialty: High-resolution esophageal manometry: Elevated upper esophageal sphincter pressure 180mmHg (normal 40-80mmHg). Incomplete and incoordinated cricopharyngeal relaxation during swallowing — cricopharyngeal dysfunction. Integrated relaxation pressure elevated. Raised intraluminal pressure above cricopharyngeus forces mucosa to herniate through Killian's dehiscence — creating the diverticulum. Treatment: Endoscopic or surgical cricopharyngeal myotomy with diverticulectomy/diverticulopexy. NOTE: Do NOT perform upper endoscopy aggressively — the scope can perforate the thin-walled diverticulum if passed blindly.

hx_brief: 26yo male with a history of Crohn's disease presents with 5 days of profuse watery diarrhea and fever after starting a new medication 3 weeks ago.

hints.hx: Crohn's disease well-controlled on azathioprine for 2 years. His rheumatologist recently added doxycycline for a skin infection — then ciprofloxacin when doxycycline didn't fully clear it. Now has 18 watery stools per day. Lower abdominal cramping waking him from sleep. He assumes it's a 'Crohn's flare' — but his Crohn's has never involved this pattern. No recent hospitalizations. No prior C. diff. The Crohn's itself is not a major risk factor here — the antibiotics are.
hints.pe: Temperature 38.9C. HR 116. BP 104/68. Diffuse lower abdominal tenderness — most severe in left lower quadrant. No guarding or rebound (not yet). Abdomen moderately distended. Hypoactive bowel sounds.
hints.img: CT Abdomen/Pelvis: Pancolonic wall thickening from cecum to rectum. 'Accordion sign' — mucosal edema and haustral thickening. Pericolic fat stranding. No pneumatosis. No free air. Transverse colon diameter 7.2cm — approaching toxic megacolon. No terminal ileal thickening (helps distinguish from Crohn's flare).

lab_results.CBC: WBC 34,800 (critically high — WBC >30K predicts fulminant course). Bands 26% (high).
lab_results.CMP: Albumin 2.0 (critically low — poor prognostic marker). Creatinine 2.4 (high — up from baseline 0.9). Na 130 (low). Lactate 2.8 mmol/L (high).
lab_results.Specialty: C. difficile NAAT/PCR: Positive. GDH (glutamate dehydrogenase) antigen: Positive. Toxin A/B immunoassay: Positive. Fecal lactoferrin: Markedly elevated (mucosal inflammation). Stool WBC: 40/hpf. Flexible sigmoidoscopy: Yellow-white pseudomembranes adherent to colonic mucosa — biopsies taken. Biopsy: Superficial mucosal necrosis with 'volcano' lesions — fibrinopurulent exudate erupting from crypts. Stool culture: No Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli O157. Abdominal CT: Pancolitis with marked wall thickening, thumbprinting — near-megacolon.

hx_brief: 58yo female presents to her hepatologist after her PCP noted elevated alkaline phosphatase on a routine panel. She admits to fatigue and itching for the past 14 months — she had not mentioned it to her doctor.

hints.hx: Itching started insidiously — she attributed it to dry skin. Progresses throughout the day, worst at night, unresponsive to moisturizers and antihistamines. Dark urine and lighter stools over the past 8 months. Profound fatigue — can barely work afternoon shifts. Dry eyes — uses lubricating drops 4-5x daily. Dry mouth — needs water to swallow food. No alcohol. No hepatitis risk factors. No medications before the itching. Associated autoimmune conditions: Sjögren's and autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's).
hints.pe: Scleral icterus — moderate. Xanthelasma around eyelids. Xanthomas on extensor surfaces of elbows and knees. Excoriations on arms and trunk from chronic scratching. Hepatomegaly 4cm. No splenomegaly yet. No ascites. No spider angiomata.
hints.img: MRCP: No biliary obstruction. Normal intra and extrahepatic bile ducts. No choledocholithiasis. No pancreatic mass. CT Abdomen: Hepatomegaly, no focal lesions. No portal hypertension. Liver elastography: E 9.8 kPa (elevated — significant fibrosis, F3).

lab_results.CMP: ALP 980 (critically high — cholestatic pattern, >3x ULN). GGT 720 (critically high). Total bilirubin 8.4 (high). Direct bilirubin 6.2 (high). AST 88 (high), ALT 72 (high). Total IgM: markedly elevated (high).
lab_results.UA: Bilirubin: Strongly positive (conjugated hyperbilirubinemia — conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble, appears in urine). Urobilinogen: Low (reduced bile reaching gut → less enterohepatic recycling).
lab_results.Specialty: AMA (anti-mitochondrial antibody): Positive at 1:2560 — M2 subtype (directed against pyruvate dehydrogenase complex E2 subunit). Anti-sp100: Positive. Anti-gp210: Positive. ANA: Positive, rim pattern. ANCA: Negative. Liver biopsy: Florid duct lesion — lymphocytic infiltration and granulomatous destruction of interlobular bile ducts. Periportal fibrosis stage II. No hepatitis B or C. Ceruloplasmin: Normal (Wilson's excluded). Alpha-1 antitrypsin: Normal.

hx_brief: 58yo male presents with 18 months of progressive leg weakness, gait difficulty, and urinary incontinence. He has been treated for lumbar spinal stenosis with 2 epidural injections — no improvement. MRI lumbar spine: mild degenerative changes only.

hints.hx: Weakness started in the left leg — foot drop — then spread to the right. He describes it as 'my legs feel heavy and won't do what I tell them.' Gait: wide-based, stiff. He cannot walk more than 100 feet. Urinary incontinence for 8 months. No bowel incontinence. No saddle anesthesia. No trauma. No cancer history. No back surgery. The progression is GRADUAL over 18 months — not acute. Pain is burning and lancinating down both legs.
hints.pe: Lower extremity weakness: 3/5 bilateral. Upper extremities: 5/5. Hyperreflexia in lower extremities — UMN pattern. Bilateral Babinski present. Sensory: loss of vibration and proprioception in both feet — level at T10 on testing. No saddle anesthesia (distinguishes from cauda equina). Dilated superficial veins visible on the lower back and buttocks.
hints.img: MRI thoracic spine with gadolinium: Serpiginous flow voids along the dorsal surface of the thoracic cord at T6-T11 — engorged vessels. Cord signal change T8-T10 on T2 — edema from venous hypertension. No disc herniation. No tumor. No compression. Spinal angiography (gold standard): Abnormal arteriovenous fistula at T9 — single feeding artery from right T9 intercostal artery draining into dilated perimedullary veins.

lab_results.CMP: Normal.
lab_results.UA: Normal — no infection causing the incontinence.
lab_results.Specialty: Spinal digital subtraction angiography: Abnormal arteriovenous connection identified at T9 — single feeding artery from right T9 intercostal artery draining into dilated perimedullary veins. Venous hypertension pattern — retrograde flow in the coronal venous plexus. Venous pressure measurements: Perimedullary venous pressure 74% of arterial (normal <50%). No intramedullary nidus. No high-flow AVM. Embolization performed: Onyx injection — complete obliteration of fistulous point confirmed on immediate post-embolization run.

hx_brief: 21yo female presents with 8 days of bilateral lower abdominal pain, fever, and vaginal discharge. She was diagnosed with a UTI at student health and prescribed nitrofurantoin 7 days ago — no improvement.

hints.hx: New sexual partner 5 weeks ago. Inconsistent barrier protection. Urinalysis at student health: 2 WBCs/hpf, no nitrites, no bacteria — 'mild pyuria' misinterpreted as UTI. Pain is bilateral, worsens with walking and intercourse (deep dyspareunia). Yellowish vaginal discharge started 4 days ago. No prior STIs. Menstrual cycle last ended 10 days ago — not pregnant.
hints.pe: Temperature 38.4C. HR 106. Bilateral lower quadrant tenderness — right slightly worse than left. Pelvic exam: Mucopurulent endocervical discharge (yellow-green). Cervical motion tenderness strongly positive (chandelier sign — patient jumps with gentle cervical manipulation). Bilateral adnexal tenderness — right adnexa fullness suggests tubo-ovarian complex.
hints.img: Transvaginal ultrasound: Bilateral thickened, fluid-filled fallopian tubes (hydrosalpinx pattern). Right adnexa: 3.8cm complex mass with internal echoes — early tubo-ovarian abscess. Free fluid in cul-de-sac. MRI Pelvis: Bilateral salpingitis confirmed. No TOA rupture (no free air).

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.UA: Normal — definitively no infection. No WBCs, no nitrites. The prior 'mild pyuria' was likely contamination from vaginal secretions. Urinary tract is not involved.
lab_results.Specialty: Cervical NAAT: Chlamydia trachomatis POSITIVE. Neisseria gonorrhoeae: POSITIVE (concurrent). Rectal NAAT: Gonorrhea positive. Throat NAAT: Negative. HIV rapid test: Negative. RPR: Negative. Chlamydia IgG: Positive (prior or current exposure). The UTI misdiagnosis delayed treatment by 7 days — risk of tubal scarring and future ectopic pregnancy/infertility increases with delayed treatment. Hospitalization for IV cefoxitin + doxycycline (TOA present). Partner notification and treatment mandatory.

hx_brief: 32yo male presents with 7 weeks of daily high spiking fevers, severe fatigue, joint pain, and a rash that only his wife has seen — it appears during his fever spikes and disappears within an hour.

hints.hx: Fevers spike to 39.5-40.8C every afternoon between 4-7pm — then normalize completely by morning (quotidian). The rash is salmon-pink, non-itchy, on his trunk — his wife says it disappears when she looks for it at other times. Multiple joints involved symmetrically — wrists, MCPs, knees, ankles. Worked up for infectious endocarditis (negative x2), lymphoma (PET negative), and SLE (ANA/dsDNA/Smith negative) — all negative. Lost 14 pounds. ESR 108mm/hr from an outside lab.
hints.pe: During 5pm exam (fever spike): Temperature 40.1C. Evanescent salmon-colored macules and papules on trunk — blanch with pressure, begin fading within 30 minutes of observation. Splenomegaly 5cm below left costal margin. Cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy (multiple nodes). Bilateral wrist and MCP synovitis.
hints.img: PET-CT: FDG uptake in lymph nodes (cervical, axillary, mediastinal, abdominal), spleen, and synovium bilaterally. No discrete mass. No bone marrow FDG uptake. Repeat bone marrow biopsy (outside): Reactive hemophagocytosis — 30% histiocytes engulfing RBCs and platelets.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 32,400 (high — predominantly neutrophils). Hgb 8.4 (low). Platelets 98,000 (low — from hemophagocytosis).
lab_results.CMP: LDH 1,480 (high). AST 108 (high), ALT 88 (high). Albumin 2.4 (low).
lab_results.Coags: Fibrinogen 88mg/dL (critically low). D-dimer markedly elevated (high) — concurrent macrophage activation syndrome.
lab_results.Specialty: Ferritin: 84,000 ng/mL (markedly elevated). Glycosylated ferritin fraction: 18% (normal >50% — markedly reduced glycosylation seen in specific hyperferritinemic states). IL-18: 48,000 pg/mL (markedly elevated — macrophage activation marker). IL-1β: Elevated. ANA: Negative. RF: Negative. Anti-CCP: Negative. ANCA: Negative. Anti-dsDNA: Negative. Blood cultures x6: No growth. Bone marrow biopsy: Reactive histiocytosis — no malignancy, no hemophagocytosis. PET-CT: Diffuse FDG uptake in spleen, liver, lymph nodes — hypermetabolic inflammatory pattern, no discrete mass.

hx_brief: 54yo male construction supervisor presents to urgent care after his wife dragged him in. He has been limping for 3 days and refuses to wear shoes. His foot has been treated with orthotics and ibuprofen by his PCP for 6 months for 'plantar fasciitis' — the orthotics have never fit because of 'the bumps on his feet.'

hints.hx: The current attack came on overnight — he woke at 3am from deep sleep to severe pain in his right foot. By morning it was red, swollen, and he could not put on his shoe. Pain is 9/10 — cannot bear weight. He drinks 3-4 beers most evenings. Takes hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension. Has had 5-6 similar attacks over 2 years, each lasting 1-2 weeks — right foot, left ankle, and once the right knee. Between attacks he's completely normal. The 'bumps' on his feet: firm, chalky white deposits over the metatarsal heads — present for 1 year. Similar deposits on his right ear helix.
hints.pe: Right first MTP joint: markedly erythematous, swollen, exquisitely tender — cannot tolerate sheet touching it. Warm to palpation. ROM severely limited. Firm whitish deposits palpable over bilateral metatarsal heads and right olecranon. Right ear helix: 3mm firm white nodule. Left ankle: mild residual swelling from prior attack.
hints.img: X-ray right foot: Soft tissue swelling at first MTP. Punched-out erosions with overhanging edges and sclerotic borders — 'rat bite' appearance at metatarsal head. Joint space preserved (distinguishes from OA and RA). Chondrocalcinosis absent. Subcortical cyst formation.

lab_results.CMP: Uric acid 11.4 mg/dL (high). Creatinine 1.6 (high). Glucose 126 (high). BUN 24 (high-normal).
lab_results.UA: Normal. 24hr urine uric acid: 420mg/day (normal 250-800 — under-excretor, not over-producer). 24hr urine volume: 820mL/day (low — promotes crystallization).
lab_results.Specialty: Aspiration of right first MTP joint (during flare from archived sample): 3.2mL turbid fluid. WBC 62,000/µL (80% PMNs — sterile inflammatory). Gram stain: Negative. Culture: Negative. Polarized light microscopy: Needle-shaped crystals — negatively birefringent (yellow when parallel to slow axis of compensator, blue when perpendicular). Distinguishes gout (negatively birefringent) from CPPD (positively birefringent — blue when parallel). Serum urate during acute attack: May be NORMAL (attack mobilizes urate, lowers serum level) — do not exclude gout based on normal serum urate during flare. Allopurinol initiated after flare resolved (never start urate-lowering therapy during an acute attack).

hx_brief: 36yo female presents to rheumatology with 5 months of proximal limb weakness, a rash on her knuckles, and a new dry cough. She was initially diagnosed with 'polymyalgia rheumatica' at age 36 — an unusual age for PMR.

hints.hx: Weakness is proximal — difficulty rising from toilet, climbing stairs, reaching overhead. No muscle pain (PMR causes pain, not weakness — wrong diagnosis initially). Knuckle rash 4 months — raised, scaly, on MCP and PIP knuckles (not on palms or dorsum of hand — specific location). New dry cough for 6 weeks — she assumed it was post-COVID. No Raynaud's. No sicca symptoms. No prior autoimmune history. No malignancy history.
hints.pe: Gottron papules: Scaly erythematous papules bilaterally over MCP and PIP extensor surfaces — pathognomonic for dermatomyositis. No heliotrope rash. V-sign: erythema in V-neckline distribution. Shawl sign: erythema over posterior shoulders. Mechanic's hands: Hyperkeratotic cracking of palmar lateral fingers. Proximal strength: 2/5 upper and lower extremities. Fine bibasilar crackles.
hints.img: MRI thighs: Diffuse T2 hyperintensity and enhancement bilateral quadriceps and hamstrings — inflammatory myopathy. HRCT Chest: Bilateral ground-glass opacities and consolidation lower lobes — organizing pneumonia pattern. No honeycombing (OP pattern rather than UIP — more treatment-responsive).

lab_results.CMP: CK 11,400 U/L (critically high). Aldolase 48 U/L (high). AST 360 (high), ALT 280 (high). LDH 820 (high).
lab_results.UA: Myoglobinuria — dipstick blood 3+ with no RBCs on microscopy.
lab_results.ABG: pO2 68 (low) on room air — ILD with OP pattern.
lab_results.Specialty: Anti-MDA5 antibody: POSITIVE (MDA5 = melanoma differentiation-associated gene 5, innate immune RNA helicase — cytoplasmic pattern on IF). Anti-Jo-1: Negative. Anti-Mi-2: Negative. Anti-TIF1γ: Negative. Anti-NXP2: Negative. Anti-SRP: Negative. HRCT chest: Bilateral ground-glass opacities, subpleural consolidation — rapidly progressive interstitial pattern. PFTs: FVC 52% predicted. DLCO 38% predicted. BAL: Lymphocytosis 42%. Muscle biopsy: Perifascicular atrophy and necrosis. Skin biopsy: Interface dermatitis with mucin deposition.

hx_brief: 51yo male with a 4-year history of peptic ulcer disease refractory to PPI therapy presents to GI after his 5th ulcer in 2 years. H. pylori has been tested and treated twice — confirmed eradicated.

hints.hx: Ulcers in unusual locations — distal duodenum and proximal jejunum (typical H. pylori ulcers are in the duodenal bulb/antrum — not distal duodenum). Chronic watery diarrhea — 8-12 stools per day regardless of what he eats. Steatorrhea — stools float and are difficult to flush. Lost 22 pounds over 2 years. Takes high-dose omeprazole (80mg twice daily) — only partial relief. Father had 'a pancreatic tumor' — operated on at age 58. H. pylori breath test confirmed negative x2.
hints.pe: Cachectic. BMI 17.6. Mild epigastric tenderness. No peritoneal signs. Mild hepatomegaly. No palpable abdominal mass.
hints.img: CT Abdomen with pancreatic protocol: Prominent gastric rugal folds. Duodenal and proximal jejunal wall thickening. 1.8cm hypodense lesion in pancreatic head — partially enhancing. Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (OctreoScan): Intense uptake in pancreatic head lesion. No hepatic metastases.

lab_results.CMP: Albumin 2.2 (low). Vitamin B12 low (low). Vitamin D severely deficient (critically low).
lab_results.Lipase: 820 U/L (high) — acid damage to pancreatic ducts.
lab_results.Coags: PT 18.2s (high) — fat-soluble vitamin K malabsorption from steatorrhea.
lab_results.Specialty: Fasting serum gastrin (PPIs held 1 week): 2,800 pg/mL (markedly elevated; normal <100). Gastric pH during fasting: 1.2 (maximally acidic — confirms autonomous acid hypersecretion). Secretin stimulation test: Gastrin paradoxically rises >200 pg/mL above baseline — positive test (normal: no rise or fall). Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (Octreoscan): Hypermetabolic lesion in pancreatic head 1.4cm and multiple hepatic foci. Endoscopic ultrasound: Pancreatic head lesion confirmed — fine needle aspiration: Neuroendocrine tumor, chromogranin A positive, synaptophysin positive, Ki-67 2%. PTH: Elevated. Pituitary MRI: 6mm adenoma found incidentally.

hx_brief: 38yo female presents to the ED with sudden severe chest pain and ST elevations in the anterior leads. She has no cardiac risk factors. She delivered her second child 3 weeks ago and has been breastfeeding.

hints.hx: Chest pain onset sudden while breastfeeding — tearing, left-sided, radiating to the jaw. She has no hypertension, no diabetes, no hyperlipidemia, no smoking history, no family history of early cardiac disease. No cocaine use. No connective tissue disorder diagnosed. She is 38, healthy, active. The timing — peripartum period — is the critical clue. Prior to this she had one episode of 'heart fluttering' during her first pregnancy.
hints.pe: BP 138/88, HR 108. Diaphoretic. Mild ST elevation visible on monitor. No Marfanoid features. No skin hyperextensibility. Normal heart sounds. Bilateral breast engorgement from breastfeeding.
hints.img: Echo: Anterior wall hypokinesis. LVEF 38%. No pericardial effusion. No valvular disease. Aortic root normal. CT aorta: No dissection flap. Normal aortic diameter throughout.

lab_results.CMP: Normal.
lab_results.EKG: ST elevation V2-V5. No reciprocal changes in inferior leads — pattern atypical for plaque rupture.
lab_results.Trop: Troponin I 42 ng/mL (critically high) — massive elevation, true MI.
lab_results.Specialty: Urgent coronary angiography: LAD shows smooth-walled narrowing over a 4cm segment — NO atherosclerotic plaque, NO thrombus visible on angiography alone. Intravascular OCT (optical coherence tomography): Intramural hematoma within the LAD wall — false lumen compressing the true lumen. Classic double lumen without communication. No intimal tear identified — Type 2 SCAD (intramural hematoma type). Fibromuscular dysplasia on renal artery imaging. Conservative management: anticoagulation avoided. EF recovered to 55% at 8 weeks.

hx_brief: 63yo male who worked as an insulation contractor for 30 years presents with 4 months of right-sided chest pain and progressive dyspnea. His PCP is treating him for COPD — he has never smoked.

hints.hx: Never smoked — critical detail. COPD diagnosis was made without spirometry or occupational history. Worked installing asbestos pipe lagging and boiler insulation until age 45 — without respiratory protection. Latency period: 18-50 years for mesothelioma (exposure stopped 18 years ago — within typical range). Right-sided chest heaviness rather than sharp pain. Weight loss 14 pounds. Night sweats. Pleural effusion drained at outside hospital — 'exudate, no malignant cells' — cytology is insensitive for mesothelioma (50-60% sensitivity).
hints.pe: Reduced expansion right hemithorax. Stony dullness right base extending to mid-lung. Absent breath sounds right lower and mid-lung. Trachea midline (large effusion but no shift — pleural rind restricting expansion). No clubbing. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No supraclavicular nodes.
hints.img: CT Chest: Right pleural effusion 1.8L. Underlying pleural rind/peel — nodular pleural thickening encasing right lung. Calcified pleural plaques bilaterally (asbestos exposure marker — bilateral even with unilateral disease). PET-CT: FDG-avid pleural thickening right hemithorax — metabolically active. No contralateral disease. No abdominal metastases.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.ABG: pO2 64 (low) on room air — lung compressed by effusion and trapped by pleural rind.
lab_results.Specialty: Thoracentesis: 1.6L bloody exudative fluid. LDH: 820 U/L. Protein: 6.2 g/dL. Cytology: Atypical mesothelial cells — insufficient alone. CT-guided pleural biopsy: Epithelioid cell proliferation with tubular and papillary architecture, dense stromal invasion. Immunohistochemistry: Calretinin positive, WT-1 positive, CK5/6 positive, D2-40 positive. TTF-1 negative, CEA negative, Ber-EP4 negative (excludes adenocarcinoma). BAP1 loss on IHC. FISH: Homozygous deletion of CDKN2A (p16). Asbestos bodies on BAL.

hx_brief: 24yo male is brought to the ED by his college roommate who says 'he's acting completely insane — this started 5 days ago.' He has no psychiatric history.

hints.hx: Roommate reports the patient had a brief flu-like illness 3 weeks ago — resolved. Five days ago: personality change, then agitation and combativeness, then bizarre behavior — stripping off clothes, hypersexuality, and episodes of staring with lip-smacking movements. Had two witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizures at home. Comprehensive toxicology screen at outside hospital: Negative. HIV negative. No drug use confirmed by collateral history.
hints.pe: Restrained in ED (agitated and combative). GCS 12 — fluctuating. Temperature 38.6C. Orofacial dyskinesias: repetitive lip-pursing, tongue-thrusting, jaw-clenching movements — stereotyped and continuous. Pupils: normal and reactive. No meningismus. No focal motor deficits. Autonomic instability: BP 168/102 (labile), HR 118 (labile), diaphoresis.
hints.img: MRI Brain FLAIR: Bilateral medial temporal lobe T2 hyperintensity (hippocampi and amygdalae) — subtle, may be missed on initial read. No mass effect. No hemorrhage. EEG: Diffuse slowing. Delta brush pattern (pathognomonic for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis when present). No ictal activity.

lab_results.CMP: Na 124 (low — SIADH from hypothalamic dysfunction).
lab_results.Specialty: CSF: Opening pressure 18 cmH2O. Lymphocytic pleocytosis 38 cells/µL. Protein 62 mg/dL (mildly elevated). Glucose: Normal. CSF antibody panel (GluN1 subunit): Positive at 1:32 titer. Serum antibody panel: Positive at 1:64. MRI brain: Subtle FLAIR hyperintensity in right hippocampus and insular cortex. EEG: Extreme delta brush pattern — rhythmic delta activity with superimposed beta — highly characteristic of this encephalitis. Pelvic MRI: 3.2cm right ovarian cystic lesion with solid components — teratoma morphology. Infectious panel (HSV, VZV, CMV, EBV, enterovirus, West Nile): All negative.

hx_brief: 48yo male presents to his PCP with 6 weeks of a nonproductive cough, fever, and fatigue that has not responded to two courses of antibiotics — azithromycin then levofloxacin.

hints.hx: Lived in the Ohio River Valley (Cincinnati area) for 22 years — moved to Phoenix, Arizona 8 months ago. Works in construction — demolition and soil excavation. The illness started 5 weeks after a large demolition project where he was working in dusty conditions. Immunocompetent — no HIV, no steroids, no biologics. Non-smoker. Two prior episodes of 'walking pneumonia' while in Ohio — now in retrospect possibly related.
hints.pe: Temperature 38.2C. HR 96. O2 sat 92% on room air. Fine crackles bilateral lower lobes. Mild hepatomegaly. Cervical lymphadenopathy. Oral exam: Three shallow, painless ulcers on buccal mucosa. Skin: two crusted papular lesions on right forearm — not typical of bacterial skin infection.
hints.img: CT Chest: Bilateral nodular infiltrates with mediastinal and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. CT Abdomen: Hepatomegaly with multiple hypodense lesions 0.5-1.5cm — hepatic involvement. No bowel abnormality. Chest X-ray: Bilateral patchy infiltrates — misread as community-acquired pneumonia on two prior visits.

lab_results.CMP: ALP 320 (high — hepatic involvement). LDH 480 (high). Albumin 2.8 (low-normal).
lab_results.ABG: pO2 72 (low) on room air. A-a gradient elevated (high).
lab_results.Specialty: Urine Histoplasma antigen: POSITIVE — titer 8.8 ng/mL (high, consistent with disseminated disease; >2ng/mL = disseminated). Serum Histoplasma antigen: POSITIVE — 6.4 ng/mL. Bronchoalveolar lavage: GMS stain — small yeast forms (2-4µm) within macrophages. Culture: Dimorphic fungus identified as Histoplasma capsulatum at 4 weeks. Serology: Complement fixation antibodies ≥1:32. DNA probe: Positive. Cross-reactivity: Histoplasma antigen cross-reacts with Blastomyces — clinical correlation required (Ohio River Valley = Histoplasma; Missouri/Mississippi = Blastomyces overlap). Treatment: Itraconazole for mild disease; amphotericin B for severe/disseminated. Prior 'walking pneumonias' were likely prior Histoplasma exposures.

hx_brief: 34yo female presents to a new gastroenterologist after being told 'everything is normal' at two other institutions. She has had 5 months of debilitating abdominal pain and cannot identify a pattern — it seems to have no trigger. She was referred after her primary care noted a history of childhood sexual abuse in her chart.

hints.hx: Pain is crampy, periumbilical and lower abdominal — variable in character. She has alternating constipation (sometimes 5 days without a bowel movement) and explosive diarrhea with urgency. Significant bloating — her abdomen distends visibly throughout the day. She has identified stress as a worsening factor. No blood in stool. No nocturnal symptoms — pain never wakes her. No weight loss. No fever. She has fibromyalgia and chronic pelvic pain (both diagnosed previously). She is a high-functioning attorney who works 70-hour weeks. Her prior workup included: colonoscopy (normal), CT abdomen (normal), celiac serology (negative), thyroid (normal), inflammatory markers (normal), stool cultures x3 (negative), fecal calprotectin 22.
hints.pe: BP 118/76, HR 80. Abdomen: diffuse mild tenderness to deep palpation — no guarding, no rigidity, no peritoneal signs. Tenderness is non-specific, reproduces her usual pain. No organomegaly. No masses. Normal bowel sounds. Rectal exam: normal tone, no masses, no blood.
hints.img: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (done today at her request): No bowel wall thickening. No mesenteric adenopathy. No mass. No obstruction. No inflammatory change. Normal appendix. Normal ovaries. Uterus normal. No free fluid. Functional constipation pattern — stool burden in ascending colon.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: Fecal calprotectin: 22 µg/g (normal; <50 µg/g essentially rules out IBD — calprotectin >250 is significantly elevated). Fecal lactoferrin: Negative (no mucosal inflammation). Fecal immunochemical test (FIT): Negative (no occult blood). Bile acid malabsorption testing (SeHCAT): Normal retention (no BAM). Hydrogen breath test: Negative for SIBO and lactose intolerance. Rome IV criteria (by interview): Recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week for >3 months, associated with ≥2 of: 1) Related to defecation, 2) Change in stool frequency, 3) Change in stool form. Criteria met — IBS-Mixed type. IBS is a diagnosis of exclusion — positive diagnosis requires Rome IV criteria AND absence of alarm features AND negative alarm biomarkers. Treatment: Low-FODMAP diet, soluble fiber, peppermint oil, psychotherapy (CBT most evidence-based given trauma history).

hx_brief: 62yo retired teacher presents to neurology with 8 months of progressive difficulty swallowing, nasal speech, and bilateral hand weakness. His wife says 'he's wasting away.'

hints.hx: First noticed slurred, nasal speech 8 months ago — 'like I have a cold all the time, but I don't.' Then dysphagia — first liquids, then solids. Now chokes on water daily — aspiration risk. Bilateral hand weakness: dropped a full coffee pot last week. Fasciculations in his arms he can see at rest. No sensory symptoms — 'I can feel my hands perfectly.' No bowel or bladder involvement. No family history. Retired teacher — no occupational exposures. Military service at age 18 — Korean demilitarized zone.
hints.pe: Tongue fasciculations and atrophy — easily visible on extended tongue. Dysarthria — spastic-flaccid mixed quality (bulbar involvement). Jaw jerk: Brisk (UMN). Palatal elevation: Reduced bilaterally. Gag reflex: Preserved but delayed. Upper extremity: Atrophy bilateral thenar muscles. Fasciculations bilateral forearms. Strength 2/5 grip bilaterally. DTRs: Paradoxically hyperreflexic (brisk in context of atrophy = pathognomonic for combined UMN+LMN). Bilateral Babinski positive. Sensation: Intact in all modalities.
hints.img: MRI Brain and Spine: Bilateral corticospinal tract T2 signal — from internal capsule through cerebral peduncles to anterior horns (degeneration visible). No focal mass. No compression. No syrinx. Tongue: Atrophy and T2 signal change in brainstem motor nuclei.

lab_results.CMP: Normal. CK 480 U/L (high — mildly elevated from denervation).
lab_results.ABG: FVC 62% predicted (low — respiratory muscle involvement). NIF -42 cmH2O (borderline low).
lab_results.Specialty: EMG: Fibrillations and positive sharp waves in bilateral upper limbs, lower limbs, thoracic paraspinals, and tongue — 4 body regions. Giant motor unit potentials (chronic reinnervation). Fasciculation potentials. Motor NCS: Normal velocities, no conduction block. Sensory NCS: Normal throughout. Repetitive nerve stimulation: No decrement (excludes NMJ disorder). CSF: Normal. Anti-ganglioside antibodies: Negative (excludes MMN). Brain MRI: Corticospinal tract T2 signal — upper motor neuron degeneration. C9orf72 repeat expansion: POSITIVE — hexanucleotide repeat expansion found.

hx_brief: 62yo male presents with 2 years of progressive exertional dyspnea, bilateral submandibular gland swelling, and a new pancreatic mass found incidentally on CT. He was treated for Sjogren's syndrome for 18 months — ANA and SSA/SSB negative.

hints.hx: Salivary gland swelling is bilateral, painless, progressive — not episodic. Dyspnea is exertional — PFTs show a restrictive pattern. He has had two kidney stones in the past 3 years (calcium oxalate). He was told he has 'autoimmune pancreatitis' but never formally worked up. Orbital pseudotumor treated with steroids 4 years ago — dramatic response but recurred off steroids. He also has a history of retroperitoneal fibrosis. No dry eyes, no dry mouth despite gland swelling.
hints.pe: Bilateral submandibular gland enlargement — firm, non-tender, non-fluctuant. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. No wheeze. Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness. No hepatosplenomegaly. Eyes: bilateral proptosis mild.
hints.img: CT chest: Bilateral pulmonary infiltrates — ground glass and interstitial thickening. Mediastinal soft tissue thickening around the aorta. CT abdomen: Pancreatic mass — sausage-shaped, diffuse enlargement of the pancreatic head, capsule-like rim of hypoattenuation. Retroperitoneal fibrosis. Bilateral renal cortical lesions — wedge-shaped. Salivary gland biopsy sent.

lab_results.CMP: Cr 1.9 (high — renal involvement). Normal LFTs. Total protein 9.8 (high).
lab_results.Lipase: Normal (despite pancreatic mass — autoimmune pancreatitis often has normal lipase).
lab_results.Specialty: Serum IgG4: 840 mg/dL (markedly elevated — normal <135 mg/dL). IgG4:total IgG ratio: 42% (elevated). Submandibular gland biopsy: Dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate. Storiform fibrosis pattern. Obliterative phlebitis. IgG4+ plasma cells: >30 per high-power field. IgG4:IgG ratio on tissue: >40%. ANA: Negative. SSA/SSB: Negative. ANCA: Negative. Salivary gland biopsy: No ductal destruction (distinguishes from Sjogren's). Pancreatic mass FNA: Fibroinflammatory tissue — no malignant cells. PET: Diffuse FDG uptake in pancreas, salivary glands, kidneys, retroperitoneum — no discrete hypermetabolic mass.

hx_brief: 43yo male with HIV (CD4 420, viral load undetectable on ART) presents with 10 weeks of progressive fatigue, weight loss, and multiple episodes of jaundice. He was told 'your HIV is controlled — this must be something else.'

hints.hx: On tenofovir/emtricitabine/dolutegravir for 4 years — fully suppressed. CD4 nadir was 88 (5 years ago). Originally from Indianapolis — worked in construction for 15 years. Jaundice has been episodic — appears then improves partially. Night sweats. Lost 16 pounds over 10 weeks. Cough, mild, non-productive. No known exposures. Multiple antibiotic courses for presumed 'bacterial pneumonia' at outside facility — no improvement. Skin: two crusted nodular lesions on face.
hints.pe: Temperature 38.4C. Jaundiced. HR 98. O2 sat 91% on room air. Bilateral fine crackles. Hepatomegaly 6cm below right costal margin — tender. Splenomegaly 8cm. Diffuse lymphadenopathy — cervical, axillary, inguinal. Oropharynx: Painless shallow ulcer on palate.
hints.img: CT Chest/Abdomen: Bilateral pulmonary nodules and patchy infiltrates. Mediastinal and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Hepatomegaly with multiple hypodense lesions throughout. Splenomegaly. No bowel lesions. Bone marrow uptake on PET.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 2.8 (low — leukopenia, marrow infiltration). Hgb 8.2 (low). Platelets 62,000 (low — marrow infiltration + hypersplenism).
lab_results.CMP: ALP 480 (high). Total bilirubin 4.8 (high). Direct 3.6 (high). AST 88 (high), ALT 72 (high). Albumin 2.4 (low). LDH 820 (high).
lab_results.ABG: pO2 68 (low) on room air. A-a gradient 28 (high).
lab_results.Specialty: Urine Histoplasma antigen: POSITIVE — 12.4 ng/mL (high titer, consistent with disseminated disease). Serum Histoplasma antigen: POSITIVE — 9.8 ng/mL. BAL: GMS stain — small yeast forms (2-4µm) engulfed by macrophages. Lysis-centrifugation blood cultures: Histoplasma capsulatum growing at day 9 (bone marrow-level dissemination — extremely high fungal burden). Bone marrow biopsy: Marked histiocyte infiltration with intracellular yeast — consistent with disseminated histoplasmosis. CD4 420 does not fully protect against Histoplasma in endemic zones — unlike most opportunistic infections (CD4 <200 threshold), Histoplasma can disseminate even with CD4 >200 in heavily exposed immunosuppressed patients. Treatment: IV amphotericin B liposomal (severe disseminated disease) for 2 weeks → step-down to itraconazole for 12 months minimum.
